data.content:
This is an **Android tombstone** (not an Apple .ips file), so the Apple symbolication skill doesn't apply. Let me analyze the crash and attempt to symbolicate the .NET runtime frames manually.

**Quick summary:**
- **App:** `com.companyname.MauiTest` (MAUI, arm64)
- **Signal:** SIGABRT — abort message: `"not compatible"`
- **Memory dump** reveals: `"some intentional crash on startup."`
- **Frames #01–#07** are in `libmonosgen-2.0.so` (Mono/.NET runtime)
- **Frame #08** is anonymous/JIT (managed code)

Let me try to fetch debug symbols from the Microsoft symbol server using the BuildId.

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.command:
cd /tmp/sv-22004f42804840e19a72209681ae9cc2 && \
BUILDID="1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be" && \
# Try Microsoft symbol server - ELF buildid sym format
curl -sI "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libmonosgen-2.0.so/elf-buildid-sym-${BUILDID}/libmonosgen-2.0.so" | head -5 && \
echo "---" && \
# Try _.debug format
curl -sI "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/_.debug/elf-buildid-sym-${BUILDID}/_.debug" | head -5
arguments.description: Check Microsoft symbol server for debug symbols

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Check Microsoft symbol server for debug symbols

data.reasoningText:
This is an Android tombstone/crash log (not an Apple .ips crash log), so the `apple-crash-symbolication` skill is not appropriate here. Let me analyze this manually.

The crash is from a .NET MAUI app (`com.companyname.MauiTest`) running on Android arm64. Let me break down what I see:

**Crash Summary:**
- Signal: SIGABRT (signal 6)
- Abort message: "not compatible"
- Memory near x14 shows: "some intentional crash on startup."
- App: com.companyname.MauiTest
- ABI: arm64


- Process crashed almost immediately after startup

The backtrace shows the abort originated in libc, followed by several frames from the Mono/.NET runtime (`libmonosgen-2.0.so`), then what appears to be JIT-compiled managed code in anonymous memory. To properly symbolicate those runtime frames, the user would need the matching debug symbols for that specific build of the Mono runtime.

The BuildId `1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be` is the key to finding the right symbols. On Android, tools like `llvm-symbolizer` or `addr2line` from the NDK can help with symbolication, and Microsoft publishes .NET runtime symbols on their symbol server that can be queried using this BuildId. The Microsoft symbol server uses a specific URL pattern for ELF files based on the BuildId. I'll try downloading the debug symbols using that format and then use tools like `llvm-symbolizer` or `addr2line` to resolve the addresses.

arguments.command:
cd /tmp/sv-22004f42804840e19a72209681ae9cc2 && \
BUILDID="1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be" && \
# Try Microsoft symbol server - ELF buildid sym format
curl -sI "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libmonosgen-2.0.so/elf-buildid-sym-${BUILDID}/libmonosgen-2.0.so" | head -5 && \
echo "---" && \
# Try _.debug format
curl -sI "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/_.debug/elf-buildid-sym-${BUILDID}/_.debug" | head -5
arguments.description: Check Microsoft symbol server for debug symbols

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-22004f42804840e19a72209681ae9cc2 && \\\nBUILDID=\"1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be\" && \\\n# Try Microsoft symbol server - ELF buildid sym format\ncurl -sI \"https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libmonosgen-2.0.so/elf-buildid-sym-${BUILDID}/libmonosgen-2.0.so\" | head -5 && \\\necho \"---\" && \\\n# Try _.debug format\ncurl -sI \"https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/_.debug/elf-buildid-sym-${BUILDID}/_.debug\" | head -5", "description": "Check Microsoft symbol server for debug symbols", "initial_wait": 15}

toolArgs.command:
cd /tmp/sv-22004f42804840e19a72209681ae9cc2 && \
BUILDID="1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be" && \
# Try Microsoft symbol server - ELF buildid sym format
curl -sI "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libmonosgen-2.0.so/elf-buildid-sym-${BUILDID}/libmonosgen-2.0.so" | head -5 && \
echo "---" && \
# Try _.debug format
curl -sI "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/_.debug/elf-buildid-sym-${BUILDID}/_.debug" | head -5
toolArgs.description: Check Microsoft symbol server for debug symbols

toolCalls.name: bash

toolArgs.command: which llvm-symbolizer addr2line llvm-addr2line 2>/dev/null || (apt list --installed 2>/dev/null | grep -i llvm | head -5)

arguments.command:
cd /tmp/sv-22004f42804840e19a72209681ae9cc2 && \
# Symbolicate all libmonosgen-2.0.so frames (#01-#07)
ADDRS="0x222098 0x2241ac 0x1abc80 0x1abd14 0x16dfa4 0x16db6c 0x16de04"
for addr in $ADDRS; do
  echo "=== Frame at $addr ==="
  llvm-symbolizer --obj=mono_debug.sym --output-style=GNU "$addr"
  echo ""
done

data.content:
Symbolication complete. Here's the fully resolved backtrace:

```
#00  abort                                     libc.so
#01  ves_icall_System_Environment_FailFast     icall.c:6244
#02  ves_icall_System_Environment_FailFast_raw icall-def.h:202
#03  do_icall                                  interp/interp.c:2457
#04  get_interp_to_native_trampoline           interp/interp.c:1732
#05  handle_isinst                             type-checking.c:0
#06  handle_isinst                             type-checking.c:506
#07  handle_isinst                             type-checking.c:528
#08  <JIT compiled managed code>               (anonymous)
```

**Analysis:** The crash originated from managed code (frame #08) performing a type check (`handle_isinst` — the `is`/`as` operator in C#). This led into `Environment.FailFast()`, which called native `abort()`. Combined with the abort message `"not compatible"` and the memory dump `"some intentional crash on startup"`, this looks like an **intentional FailFast** triggered by a type compatibility check failure during app startup.

Let me clean up the downloaded symbols.

Done. The root cause is a **type compatibility check failure** (`handle_isinst`) that triggered `Environment.FailFast("not compatible")` on startup. To investigate further, you'd want to look at your managed startup code for any `is`/`as` casts or type checks that could fail and call `FailFast`.
